passages.explanation: Wanda's breathless excitement after seeing the Greek reveals something she has never shown Severin: genuine, involuntary desire. Her flushed cheeks and rapid pacing are physical symptoms of being overwhelmed, a stark contrast to the calculated cruelty she normally displays. Sacher-Masoch shows the dominatrix encountering a force that disrupts her own performance of control.

Wanda commands Severin to investigate the stranger, using her slave as an instrument of her own infatuation. The cruelty here is not theatrical but casual; she does not even register Severin's pain as she rhapsodizes about the Greek's beauty. Her taunt that he will enjoy being punished by this man reveals how thoroughly she has internalized the masochistic framework she once merely played at.

Severin gathers intelligence on Alexis Papadopolis with the efficiency of a spy and the despair of a condemned man. Each detail he reports, the Greek's atheism, his cruelty in battle, his youth, builds a portrait of everything Severin is not. Wanda's response, 'all in all, then, a man,' is a devastating verdict on Severin by implication.

Wanda's sharp dismissal of the Greek's wealth and her focus on whether he has a wife or mistress reveal her priorities: she wants possession, not luxury. Her rapid-fire questions have the quality of military reconnaissance. Sacher-Masoch shows that desire, even in the dominatrix, is a form of vulnerability masquerading as strategy.

The command to secure theater tickets and the threat of the whip for hesitation reduce Severin to a purely functional instrument. He must arrange the very date that will deepen his rival's advantage. Sacher-Masoch captures a particular refinement of cruelty: forcing the victim to labor toward his own displacement.

At the theater, Severin stands in the lobby while Wanda and the Greek devour each other with their eyes, the stage performance forgotten. His question 'what was I at that moment?' marks a shift from erotic degradation to genuine irrelevance. The masochist's deepest fear is not punishment but being forgotten entirely.

At the ambassador's ball, Wanda's weary, regal calm contrasts with her earlier feverish agitation. She has transformed herself into a weapon aimed at the Greek. Meanwhile Severin kisses her discarded furs, worshipping the residue of her presence. Sacher-Masoch draws a devastating portrait of how obsessive love reduces a person to fetishizing traces.

The Greek arrives in sable-trimmed velvet and fixes Severin with an uncomfortably long stare. Severin feels 'mortal fear' and recognizes his own inferiority as something almost physical. The Greek's aristocratic nod commanding Severin to take his furs is the first taste of a new power dynamic: Severin is now servant to both his mistress and her future master.

Severin's fevered imaginings while waiting in the anteroom are more vivid than any reality. The servants' gossip about the Greek, his vanity, his androgynous beauty, his rejection of a male suitor, builds a portrait of someone who weaponizes beauty as deliberately as Wanda does. Sacher-Masoch doubles the Venus figure: the Greek is another idol demanding worship.

The Greek's parable of the lioness who abandons her defeated mate for the victor is the chapter's thematic key. It frames female sexuality as aligned with power rather than loyalty. Wanda's quick glance at Severin confirms she recognizes herself in the story. The red dawn that 'immerses' all three of them 'in blood' signals that this triangle will end in destruction.

After the ball, Wanda stares into the fire while Severin wanders the predawn garden, pressing his forehead against cold marble. He realizes everything before was 'child's play' and genuine catastrophe approaches. Sacher-Masoch captures the moment fantasy collides with reality: the masochist designed his own suffering but never truly anticipated losing the beloved to someone who does not play by the game's rules.

Wanda praying before the statue of Venus is a pivotal image. The woman who has played the goddess now supplicates before the original, asking for something she cannot command: the Greek's love. Sacher-Masoch reveals the irony at the heart of the power dynamic: the all-powerful despot is herself enslaved by desire for a man she cannot control.

Severin's nocturnal intrusion into Wanda's bedroom, weeping over her sleeping body, is simultaneously tender and invasive. His description of lying frozen beside her 'turned into stone by horrible torments' captures the paralysis of someone who can neither act nor withdraw. The red oil lamp beneath a saint's image connects his vigil to religious devotion.

Wanda wakes and responds with unexpected compassion, stroking his hair and admitting she cannot help him. Her confession that the Greek has made an impression she 'cannot yet analyse' echoes Severin's own irrational devotion. Sacher-Masoch suggests that everyone in this triangle is a slave to something, and that the dominatrix's power was always an illusion concealing her own susceptibility.

Severin appeals to Wanda's reputation as a last resort, invoking social convention against the force of desire. Her declaration that she wants to become the Greek's wife inverts the entire power structure: the dominatrix yearns to submit. Her contemptuous dismissal of Severin's grief shows that compassion and cruelty coexist in the same person, separated only by the direction of desire.

Severin threatens suicide with a dagger; Wanda yawns and says 'let me go to sleep.' This is perhaps the most devastating gesture in the novel. His grand romantic despair bores her. Sacher-Masoch shows that the masochist's ultimate humiliation is not pain but irrelevance: when suffering itself fails to command attention, the entire economy of the relationship collapses.

The scene ends with Wanda whipping Severin out of her bedroom while he clutches a dagger and strikes a 'theatrical attitude' that she finds laughable. His own recognition that the moment is 'droll' introduces a flicker of self-awareness that foreshadows his eventual cure. Even at his most desperate, Severin cannot escape the sense that his suffering is partly performance.
